question_text: The human heart is a:
explanation: The human heart is a muscular organ with four chambers: two upper chambers called atria (left and right) and two lower chambers called ventricles (left and right). It is myogenic in origin, meaning the heartbeat is initiated by the heart muscle itself (specifically the SA node). The four-chambered heart ensures complete separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which is an adaptation for endothermy (warm-bloodedness) in mammals and birds.

elimination_hints.hint: A two-chambered heart (one atrium, one ventricle) is found in fish, not humans.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fish heart with mammalian heart

elimination_hints.hint: A three-chambered heart (two atria, one ventricle) is found in amphibians. Humans have four chambers.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing amphibian heart with mammalian heart

elimination_hints.hint: No vertebrate has a five-chambered heart. The maximum is four chambers, found in mammals and birds.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing a non-existent heart structure

question_text: The pacemaker of the heart is the:
explanation: The sino-atrial node (SA node) is called the pacemaker of the heart because it generates the electrical impulse that initiates each heartbeat. It is located in the right atrium near the opening of the superior vena cava. The SA node has the highest rate of depolarisation among all cardiac conduction tissues (about 70-75 impulses per minute), which sets the normal heart rhythm. The AV node, Bundle of His, and Purkinje fibres are parts of the cardiac conduction system but they conduct impulses initiated by the SA node.

elimination_hints.hint: The AV node receives impulses from the SA node and relays them to the ventricles. It has a slower intrinsic rate (~40-60/min) and serves as a backup pacemaker, not the primary one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the AV node with the SA node

elimination_hints.hint: The Bundle of His conducts impulses from the AV node to the ventricles via the interventricular septum. It does not initiate the heartbeat.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing conduction pathway with pacemaker

elimination_hints.hint: Purkinje fibres distribute the impulse to the ventricular myocardium, causing ventricular contraction. They do not initiate the heartbeat.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the end of the conduction pathway with its origin

question_text: Which chamber of the heart receives deoxygenated blood from the body?
explanation: The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body through two large veins: the superior vena cava (carrying blood from the head, neck, upper limbs, and thorax) and the inferior vena cava (carrying blood from the abdomen, pelvis, and lower limbs). Additionally, the coronary sinus drains deoxygenated blood from the heart wall itself into the right atrium. From the right atrium, blood passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle.

elimination_hints.hint: The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs via the pulmonary veins, not deoxygenated blood from the body.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing left and right sides of the heart

elimination_hints.hint: The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the body through the aorta. It does not receive blood from the body.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing receiving and pumping chambers

elimination_hints.hint: The right ventricle receives blood from the right atrium and pumps it to the lungs. It does not directly receive blood from the body.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the receiving chamber (atrium) with the pumping chamber (ventricle)

question_text: The valve present between the left atrium and left ventricle is the:
explanation: The bicuspid valve (also called the mitral valve) is located between the left atrium and left ventricle. It has two cusps or flaps. The tricuspid valve (three cusps) is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. These valves are collectively called atrioventricular (AV) valves. They prevent the backflow of blood from the ventricles to the atria during ventricular contraction (systole). The semilunar valves are present at the base of the aorta and pulmonary artery.

elimination_hints.hint: The tricuspid valve is between the RIGHT atrium and RIGHT ventricle, not the left side.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing right-side and left-side AV valves

elimination_hints.hint: The aortic semilunar valve is at the opening of the aorta from the left ventricle, not between the atrium and ventricle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing semilunar valves with AV valves

elimination_hints.hint: The pulmonary semilunar valve is at the opening of the pulmonary artery from the right ventricle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the location of the pulmonary valve

question_text: The wall of the left ventricle is thicker than the right ventricle because:
explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest wall (about 3 times thicker than the right ventricle) because it pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body through the systemic circulation, which requires much higher pressure. The systemic circuit is longer and has greater resistance than the pulmonary circuit. The right ventricle only needs to pump blood to the nearby lungs through the pulmonary circulation, which is a low-pressure circuit. Thus, the left ventricle must generate a higher systolic pressure (~120 mmHg) compared to the right ventricle (~25 mmHg).

options.text: It receives more blood than the right ventricle
It pumps blood to the entire body at higher pressure
It has more heart valves than the right ventricle
It contains more cardiac muscle fibres for oxygen storage

elimination_hints.hint: Both ventricles receive and pump the same volume of blood per beat (stroke volume). The difference is in the pressure required, not volume.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing volume with pressure

elimination_hints.hint: Both ventricles have the same number of associated valves (one AV valve at input, one semilunar valve at output). Wall thickness is not related to valve number.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing a non-existent difference in valve count

elimination_hints.hint: Cardiac muscle does not store oxygen. The thicker wall is an adaptation for generating higher contractile force, not storage.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing muscle thickness with oxygen storage

question_text: The correct sequence of blood flow through the heart is:
explanation: Blood flow through the heart follows this pathway: Deoxygenated blood enters the right atrium via the vena cavae → passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle → is pumped through the pulmonary semilunar valve into the pulmonary artery → goes to the lungs for gas exchange → oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium via pulmonary veins → passes through the bicuspid valve into the left ventricle → is pumped through the aortic semilunar valve into the aorta → goes to the body. This describes double circulation: pulmonary (heart→lungs→heart) and systemic (heart→body→heart).

options.text: Right atrium → Right ventricle → Pulmonary artery → Lungs → Pulmonary vein → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta
Left atrium → Left ventricle → Pulmonary artery → Lungs → Pulmonary vein → Right atrium → Right ventricle → Aorta
Right atrium → Left atrium → Right ventricle → Left ventricle → Lungs → Aorta
Right ventricle → Right atrium → Lungs → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the left and right sides. Deoxygenated blood enters the RIGHT side first, not the left side.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the left-right flow

elimination_hints.hint: Blood flows from atria to ventricles sequentially on each side, not from right atrium to left atrium directly. The septum separates the two sides.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking blood crosses from right atrium to left atrium

elimination_hints.hint: Blood enters the atrium first, then goes to the ventricle. Ventricles pump blood out; they do not receive blood from veins.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the atrium-ventricle sequence

question_text: Which of the following blood vessels carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart?
explanation: The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart. There are four pulmonary veins (two from each lung). This is an exception to the general rule that arteries carry oxygenated blood and veins carry deoxygenated blood. In pulmonary circulation, the pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs, while the pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood back to the heart. The reverse (veins carrying oxygenated blood) is unique to pulmonary circulation.

elimination_hints.hint: The pulmonary artery carries DEOXYGENATED blood FROM the heart TO the lungs. It's the opposite direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pulmonary artery with pulmonary vein

elimination_hints.hint: The superior vena cava carries deoxygenated blood from the upper body to the right atrium. It's part of systemic circulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing systemic and pulmonary veins

elimination_hints.hint: The aorta carries oxygenated blood FROM the heart TO the body, not from the lungs to the heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the aorta (outgoing) with pulmonary vein (incoming)

question_text: The heart is enclosed in a double-layered membrane called the:
explanation: The pericardium is a double-walled membranous sac that encloses the heart. It consists of two layers: the outer fibrous pericardium (tough, inelastic connective tissue) and the inner serous pericardium (which has two sublayers — the parietal layer and the visceral layer/epicardium). The pericardial cavity between the parietal and visceral layers contains pericardial fluid, which reduces friction during heartbeats and provides lubrication. The pericardium protects the heart, prevents over-distension, and anchors it within the thoracic cavity.

elimination_hints.hint: The pleura is the double membrane surrounding the lungs, not the heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the lung covering with the heart covering

elimination_hints.hint: The peritoneum is the membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering abdominal organs, not the heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the abdominal membrane with the cardiac membrane

elimination_hints.hint: The meninges are the three membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, not the heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the brain covering with the heart covering

question_text: Double circulation means that blood passes through the heart:
explanation: In double circulation, blood passes through the heart twice in one complete cycle through the body. The first pass is the pulmonary circulation: deoxygenated blood is pumped from the right ventricle to the lungs and oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium. The second pass is the systemic circulation: oxygenated blood is pumped from the left ventricle to the body and deoxygenated blood returns to the right atrium. This ensures that highly oxygenated blood is delivered to the body tissues, which is essential for maintaining the high metabolic rate of mammals.

options.text: Three times in each complete cycle
Blood does not pass through the heart at all

elimination_hints.hint: Blood passing once through the heart is single circulation, found in fish with their two-chambered heart. Mammals have double circulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing single circulation (fish) with double circulation (mammals)

elimination_hints.hint: There is no circulatory system where blood passes through the heart three times. The term 'double' specifically means twice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing a non-existent circulation pattern

elimination_hints.hint: In closed circulatory systems like in humans, blood always passes through the heart. An open circulatory system (in insects) is different but still has a heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing open and closed circulatory systems

question_text: The coronary artery supplies blood to the:
explanation: The coronary arteries supply oxygenated blood to the heart muscle (myocardium) itself. The right and left coronary arteries arise from the base of the aorta, just above the aortic semilunar valve. The left coronary artery divides into the left anterior descending (LAD) artery and the left circumflex artery. The right coronary artery supplies the right atrium, right ventricle, and the posterior part of the left ventricle. Blockage of coronary arteries (coronary artery disease) leads to reduced blood supply to the heart muscle, causing angina pectoris or myocardial infarction (heart attack).

elimination_hints.hint: The lungs receive blood from the pulmonary arteries, not the coronary arteries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing coronary arteries with pulmonary arteries

elimination_hints.hint: The brain is supplied by the carotid arteries and vertebral arteries, not coronary arteries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing coronary arteries with carotid arteries

elimination_hints.hint: The kidneys are supplied by the renal arteries, which branch off the abdominal aorta.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing specific arterial supply to organs

question_text: The correct order of the cardiac conduction system is:
explanation: The cardiac conduction system generates and propagates electrical impulses in the following sequence: (1) SA node (located in the right atrium) generates the impulse → (2) AV node (located in the lower part of the right atrium near the atrial septum) receives and slightly delays the impulse (~0.1 seconds), allowing atria to complete their contraction before ventricles begin → (3) Bundle of His (AV bundle) carries the impulse from the AV node through the interventricular septum → (4) Bundle branches (right and left) distribute impulses to respective ventricles → (5) Purkinje fibres spread the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium, causing coordinated ventricular contraction.

options.text: SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibres
AV node → SA node → Purkinje fibres → Bundle of His
Bundle of His → SA node → AV node → Purkinje fibres
Purkinje fibres → Bundle of His → AV node → SA node

elimination_hints.hint: The SA node initiates the impulse first, not the AV node. The AV node receives the impulse from the SA node.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the roles of SA and AV nodes

elimination_hints.hint: The Bundle of His is downstream of both SA and AV nodes. The impulse originates at the SA node, not the Bundle of His.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the correct initiation point

elimination_hints.hint: This is the complete reverse of the correct sequence. The impulse starts at the SA node and ends at Purkinje fibres.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the entire conduction pathway

question_text: A patient is diagnosed with a condition where the SA node fails to function properly. Which of the following is the most likely consequence?
explanation: If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over as the pacemaker (it is the secondary or latent pacemaker). However, the AV node has a slower intrinsic rate of about 40-60 beats per minute compared to the SA node's normal rate of 70-75 beats per minute. This results in a slower but regular heart rate. In clinical practice, if both the SA and AV nodes fail, an artificial electronic pacemaker may be implanted. The Bundle of His and Purkinje fibres have even slower intrinsic rates (20-40 bpm) and would only take over if both nodes fail.

options.text: The heart rate increases to compensate
The AV node takes over as pacemaker with a slower heart rate
Only the atria stop contracting while ventricles continue normally

elimination_hints.hint: The heart does not stop immediately because the AV node can act as a backup pacemaker. Complete cardiac arrest would require failure of all conduction components.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about backup pacemaker function

elimination_hints.hint: The AV node has a slower intrinsic rate than the SA node, so the heart rate decreases, not increases.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking compensation means speeding up

elimination_hints.hint: The AV node takes over as pacemaker and still stimulates both atria and ventricles, though at a slower rate. It's not a selective effect on one chamber.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking SA node failure only affects atrial contraction

question_text:
Assertion (A): The human heart is described as myogenic.
Reason (R): The heartbeat in humans is initiated by a specialised cardiac muscle (SA node), not by nerve impulses.
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. A myogenic heart is one in which the heartbeat is initiated by the heart muscle itself. In humans, the SA node (a patch of modified cardiac muscle in the right atrium) spontaneously generates electrical impulses that trigger each heartbeat. This contrasts with a neurogenic heart (found in some arthropods), where the heartbeat is initiated by nerve impulses from a nerve ganglion. While the human heart is myogenic, its rate can be modulated by the autonomic nervous system (sympathetic increases rate; parasympathetic/vagus nerve decreases rate).

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly defines and explains why the heart is myogenic — the impulse originates in cardiac muscle (SA node), not in nerves. This is the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that the definition of myogenic is given in the Reason

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true. The SA node IS specialised cardiac muscle tissue that initiates the heartbeat without needing nerve input.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the heartbeat requires nerve impulses to start

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true. The human heart is indeed myogenic — this is a fundamental NCERT concept.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing myogenic with neurogenic

question_text:
Assertion (A): The right and left sides of the heart are completely separated by a septum in humans.
Reason (R): Complete separation prevents the mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which is essential for efficient oxygen delivery to tissues.
explanation: Both statements are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. In humans (and all mammals), the heart has a complete interventricular septum and interatrial septum that separate the right (deoxygenated) and left (oxygenated) sides. This complete separation ensures that oxygenated blood from the lungs is pumped to the body without mixing with deoxygenated blood, maintaining high oxygen concentration for efficient delivery. This is an evolutionary adaptation for endothermy (maintaining high body temperature). In contrast, amphibians have an incomplete septum, allowing partial mixing.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason explains the functional advantage of the septum — preventing mixing for efficient O₂ delivery. This IS the correct explanation for why the septum exists.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal link between structure and function

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true — complete separation does prevent mixing and is essential for maintaining metabolic rate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly rejecting a well-established physiological principle

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true — humans have a complete septum. Unlike amphibians, there is no incomplete septum in the human heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing human heart anatomy with amphibian heart anatomy

question_text:
Assertion (A): The AV node introduces a brief delay in the conduction of the cardiac impulse.
Reason (R): The delay at the AV node allows the atria to complete their contraction and empty blood into the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.
explanation: Both statements are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. The AV node introduces a delay of approximately 0.1 seconds (100 milliseconds) in the conduction of the impulse from the atria to the ventricles. This delay is physiologically critical because it allows the atria to complete their systole (contraction) and fully empty their blood into the ventricles before the ventricles begin to contract. Without this delay, the atria and ventricles would contract simultaneously, resulting in inefficient filling and reduced cardiac output.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason gives the precise physiological purpose of the AV delay — to allow complete atrial emptying. This IS the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that the purpose of the delay is to ensure proper atrial filling of ventricles

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true. Without the AV delay, atria and ventricles would contract simultaneously, reducing cardiac efficiency.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the importance of sequential contraction

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true. The AV node does introduce a measurable delay (~0.1 seconds) in impulse conduction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that the AV node delays conduction

question_text:
Match the heart chamber/vessel in Column I with the type of blood it carries in Column II:

Column I:
(a) Left ventricle
(b) Pulmonary artery
(c) Pulmonary vein
(d) Right atrium

Column II:
(i) Deoxygenated blood from the body
(ii) Oxygenated blood to the body
(iii) Deoxygenated blood to the lungs
(iv) Oxygenated blood from the lungs
explanation: (a)→(ii): The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood into the aorta, which distributes it to the entire body (systemic circulation). (b)→(iii): The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for oxygenation. (c)→(iv): The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium. (d)→(i): The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body via the superior and inferior vena cava.

elimination_hints.hint: Left ventricle pumps blood TO the body (ii), not FROM the lungs (iv). Pulmonary veins bring blood from lungs (iv), not the left ventricle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the left ventricle's output with pulmonary vein input

elimination_hints.hint: Left ventricle carries oxygenated blood (ii), not deoxygenated blood (iii). The right ventricle/pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood to lungs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the left and right sides of the heart

elimination_hints.hint: This completely reverses the oxygenation status. The left side carries oxygenated blood and the right side carries deoxygenated blood.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely reversing which side carries oxygenated vs deoxygenated blood

question_text:
Match the heart valve in Column I with its location in Column II:

Column I:
(a) Tricuspid valve
(b) Bicuspid (mitral) valve
(c) Aortic semilunar valve
(d) Pulmonary semilunar valve

Column II:
(i) Opening of pulmonary artery from right ventricle
(ii) Between right atrium and right ventricle
(iii) Opening of aorta from left ventricle
(iv) Between left atrium and left ventricle
explanation: (a)→(ii): The tricuspid valve (three cusps) is located between the right atrium and right ventricle. It prevents backflow of blood into the right atrium during right ventricular contraction. (b)→(iv): The bicuspid or mitral valve (two cusps) is between the left atrium and left ventricle. (c)→(iii): The aortic semilunar valve is at the opening of the aorta from the left ventricle, preventing blood from flowing back into the left ventricle during diastole. (d)→(i): The pulmonary semilunar valve is at the opening of the pulmonary artery from the right ventricle.

elimination_hints.hint: The tricuspid valve is on the RIGHT side (ii), not the left side (iv). The bicuspid valve is on the LEFT side (iv).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the locations of tricuspid and bicuspid valves

elimination_hints.hint: The tricuspid valve is between atrium and ventricle (ii), not at the pulmonary artery opening (i). Semilunar valves are at arterial openings.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing AV valves with semilunar valves

elimination_hints.hint: This randomly assigns valves to wrong locations. The aortic valve is at the aorta (iii), not between atrium and ventricle (ii).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the specific locations of heart valves

question_text: Statement: The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. The pulmonary artery is unique because it is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. It arises from the right ventricle and divides into the right and left pulmonary arteries, which carry deoxygenated blood to the respective lungs for oxygenation. This is an exception to the general rule that arteries carry oxygenated blood. Similarly, the pulmonary veins are the only veins that carry oxygenated blood (from lungs to left atrium).

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is correct. The pulmonary artery IS an artery that carries deoxygenated blood. It is a well-known exception to the rule that arteries carry oxygenated blood.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the general rule (arteries = oxygenated) without considering the pulmonary exception

question_text: The normal resting heart rate in an adult human is approximately _______ beats per minute.
explanation: The normal resting heart rate for an adult human is approximately 72 beats per minute (range: 60-100 bpm). This rate is set by the SA node, which generates about 70-75 electrical impulses per minute. The resting heart rate can be lower in athletes (bradycardia, <60 bpm) due to increased stroke volume from a stronger, more efficient heart. Factors that increase heart rate include exercise, stress, fever, and sympathetic nervous stimulation. Factors that decrease it include rest, sleep, and parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation.

elimination_hints.hint: 40 bpm is abnormally slow (severe bradycardia) and would indicate a conduction problem or very high athletic conditioning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing abnormal bradycardia with normal resting rate

elimination_hints.hint: 120 bpm is tachycardia, which occurs during exercise or stress. The normal resting rate is much lower.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing exercise heart rate with resting heart rate

elimination_hints.hint: 200 bpm is extremely high and would only occur during intense exercise in young individuals. Normal resting rate is about 72 bpm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Greatly overestimating normal resting heart rate

question_text: A 55-year-old man experiences sudden, severe chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw, along with sweating and breathlessness. An ECG shows abnormalities and blood tests reveal elevated cardiac enzymes (troponin). What is the most likely diagnosis, and which blood vessel is most likely affected?
explanation: The symptoms described are classic signs of a myocardial infarction (heart attack). The patient presents with acute chest pain radiating to the left arm and jaw (referred pain), diaphoresis (sweating), and dyspnoea (breathlessness). Elevated cardiac enzymes (troponin) confirm cardiac muscle damage. A myocardial infarction occurs when a coronary artery (which supplies blood to the heart muscle) becomes blocked, usually due to a blood clot forming on an atherosclerotic plaque. This leads to ischaemia (lack of blood supply) and necrosis (death) of the heart muscle supplied by that artery. The left anterior descending (LAD) artery is the most commonly affected vessel.

elimination_hints.hint: Angina pectoris causes chest pain but is reversible and does NOT elevate cardiac enzymes (no muscle death). The scenario mentions elevated troponin, indicating actual tissue damage. Also, the pulmonary artery supplies lungs, not heart muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reversible ischaemia (angina) with irreversible damage (infarction)

elimination_hints.hint: Heart failure is a chronic condition of reduced cardiac output, not an acute event with sudden chest pain and enzyme elevation. The aorta supplies the body, not the heart muscle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing acute myocardial infarction with chronic heart failure

elimination_hints.hint: Pericarditis involves inflammation of the pericardium, and its pain typically worsens with breathing. The vena cava returns blood to the heart and its blockage causes different symptoms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing pericardial pain from ischaemic pain
